acep-disability-plan.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency breaks down the ACEP disability plan for emergency physicians, including benefit caps, own-occupation limitations, and when individual coverage is necessary. 15+ years specializing in income protection for emergency medicine physicians.
acep-disability-plan.tldr: The ACEP group disability plan provides accessible coverage for emergency physicians but caps benefits significantly below what EM physicians earn in high-performing shifts and contracts. The plan uses broad medical professional definitions rather than addressing the unique demands of emergency medicine work, lacks critical riders for partial disability and income growth, and ties coverage to ACEP membership. Emergency physicians earning above $300,000, particularly those with shift-based income documentation or multiple income sources, should treat the ACEP plan as a supplement, not as primary protection.

acs-disability-plan.title: ACS Disability Insurance Plan: What Surgeons Should Know
acs-disability-plan.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency analyzes the American College of Surgeons disability plan, examining surgical own-occupation definitions, benefit caps, procedural specialty risks, and coordination with individual coverage for surgeons.
acs-disability-plan.tldr: The ACS group disability plan provides accessible coverage for surgeons through simplified enrollment but caps benefits significantly below what most surgeons earn. The plan uses broad surgical occupational language rather than distinguishing between different types of surgical work, limiting its application when surgeons face partial functional impairment. Benefit caps, missing residual disability riders, and lack of COLA protection create substantial income gaps. Surgeons earning above $200,000 should treat the ACS plan as supplemental coverage only, not primary income protection.

ada-disability-plan.title: ADA Disability Insurance Plan: What Dentists Need to Know
ada-disability-plan.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency breaks down the ADA disability plan for dentists, including benefit caps, procedural-specialty gaps, and when individual coverage is necessary. 15+ years specializing in income protection for dentists.
ada-disability-plan.tldr: The ADA group disability plan gives dentists affordable baseline coverage but caps benefits below what most practice owners and specialists earn. The plan's occupational definition may not adequately distinguish between procedural dentistry and non-clinical work. Dentists face elevated disability risk from musculoskeletal injuries, hand tremor, and repetitive strain, making specialty-specific individual coverage essential.

aicpa-disability-plan.title: AICPA Disability Insurance Plan: What CPAs Need to Know
aicpa-disability-plan.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency breaks down the AICPA disability plan for CPAs, including benefit caps, definition limitations, and when individual coverage is necessary. 15+ years specializing in income protection for CPAs and accountants.
aicpa-disability-plan.tldr: The AICPA group disability plan offers CPAs affordable baseline coverage but caps benefits below what partners and senior professionals earn. The plan uses broad accounting definitions rather than distinguishing between audit, tax, advisory, and forensic specialties. Individual supplemental coverage is essential for CPAs earning above plan caps, particularly those in partner-track or ownership roles.

ama-group-disability.title: AMA Group Disability Plan: Coverage Analysis for Physicians
ama-group-disability.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency analyzes the AMA group disability plan for physicians, including benefit caps, occupational definitions, coordination with individual coverage, and specialty-specific gaps in protection.
ama-group-disability.tldr: The AMA group disability plan provides accessible coverage for physicians through group purchasing but caps benefits well below what high-earning and specialist physicians actually earn. The plan uses broad occupational language tied to general medical practice rather than specialty-specific criteria, lacks essential riders for partial disability and inflation protection, and ties coverage to AMA membership status. Physicians earning above $200,000 should treat the AMA plan as supplemental coverage, not primary income protection.

aoa-disability-plan.title: AOA Disability Insurance Plan: What Osteopathic Physicians Need to Know
aoa-disability-plan.description: Evaluate the American Osteopathic Association disability insurance plan and understand its benefit caps, limitations, and gaps for high-earning DOs. Seaworthy Insurance Agency brings 20+ years of experience helping medical professionals optimize coverage.
aoa-disability-plan.tldr: The AOA group disability plan provides baseline coverage but has significant benefit caps ($5,000-$10,000/month depending on enrollment) that rarely match DO compensation. Own-occupation coverage gaps, mental health limitations, and portability tied to AOA membership create coverage risks for procedurally-focused DOs.

apa-disability-plan.title: APA Disability Insurance Plan: What Psychologists Need to Know
apa-disability-plan.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency breaks down the APA disability plan for psychologists, including benefit limits, mental/nervous clause implications, and when individual coverage is necessary. 15+ years specializing in income protection for psychologists.
apa-disability-plan.tldr: The APA group disability plan offers psychologists accessible baseline coverage but creates unique challenges because most psychologist disabilities involve mental health conditions subject to benefit limitation clauses. The plan's benefit caps fall short for psychologists in private practice, and the generic professional definition does not distinguish between clinical, forensic, neuropsychological, and research practice. Individual coverage with careful attention to mental/nervous limitation language is essential.

avma-disability-plan.title: AVMA Disability Insurance Plan: What Veterinarians Need to Know
avma-disability-plan.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency breaks down the AVMA disability plan for veterinarians, including benefit caps, surgical-specialty gaps, and when individual coverage is necessary. 15+ years specializing in income protection for veterinarians.
avma-disability-plan.tldr: The AVMA group disability plan provides veterinarians with accessible baseline coverage but caps benefits below what practice owners, specialists, and experienced practitioners earn. The plan uses broad veterinary definitions that do not distinguish between surgical, large-animal, and companion-animal practice. Veterinarians face elevated disability risk from animal-related injuries, musculoskeletal strain, and occupational exposure, making specialty-specific individual coverage essential.

state-medical-association-disability.title: State Medical Association Disability Plans: What Physicians Should Evaluate
state-medical-association-disability.description: Seaworthy Insurance Agency analyzes state medical association disability plans (CMA, NYSMA, TMA), comparing them to national AMA coverage, own-occupation definitions, and portability challenges across state lines. 15+ years protecting physician income.
state-medical-association-disability.tldr: State medical associations offer group disability plans with distinct advantages and limitations compared to national AMA coverage. These plans typically feature lower premiums and faster enrollment but frequently cap benefits well below physician compensation levels. The own-occupation definition often uses broad medical language rather than specialty-specific criteria, and portability becomes problematic when physicians relocate to states where their association membership may not transfer. Physicians earning above $200,000 should evaluate state plans as foundational coverage only, not complete income protection.

riders.name: Residual (Partial) Disability
riders.summary: Pays a proportional benefit when a covered disability reduces income by roughly 15-20% or more. Most disabilities are partial, not total; without this rider a policy only pays when you cannot work at all.

riders.name: Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA)
riders.summary: Increases the monthly benefit during long claims to keep pace with inflation. Compound 3% is the standard structure. Value drops materially past age 50 because the remaining benefit horizon shortens.

riders.name: Future Increase Option (FIO)
riders.summary: Locks in insurability. Lets the insured buy more coverage as income rises without new medical underwriting. Typically available until age 55 or a set number of years.

riders.name: Own-Occupation Enhancement
riders.audience: anyone with a modified-own-occ base policy
riders.summary: Extends the true own-occupation definition beyond the standard 24-month transition if the base contract is modified own-occ. Not needed when the base policy is true own-occ to age 65 by default.

riders.name: Retirement Protection
riders.audience: high earners with significant retirement-savings goals
riders.summary: Continues retirement-fund contributions during a disability. Valuable with a long career horizon and large retirement-savings targets; less valuable for late-career buyers.

riders.name: Return of Premium
riders.summary: Adds roughly 30-50% to the base premium. The opportunity cost of the extra premium invested elsewhere almost always exceeds the returned amount.

riders.name: Catastrophic Disability
riders.summary: Adds benefit for severe disabilities (loss of two or more activities of daily living). Coverage is meaningful but catastrophic disabilities are rare; cost-to-expected-value is typically unfavorable.

riders.name: Social Insurance Supplement
riders.summary: Pays if a Social Security disability claim is denied. Most high earners will not qualify for SSDI under the "any occupation" standard, so the trigger rarely fires usefully.
